steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The blank holds a title ("linguist") immediately followed by a name ("Diane Massam"). The name specifies which linguist of the University of Toronto is meant — it is essential to the meaning, making it a restrictive appositive.

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content: A restrictive appositive takes no punctuation between the title and the name (compare: "author Toni Morrison"). A colon between a noun and its renaming noun is also wrong.

steps.title: Eliminate the wrong choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘linguist, Diane Massam,’: paired commas treat the name as nonessential, violating the restrictive appositive rule. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘linguist, Diane Massam’: a single comma after "linguist" wrongly separates the title from the name it specifies. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘linguist Diane Massam’: no punctuation between title and name, matching the restrictive-appositive rule. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘linguist: Diane Massam’: a colon cannot stand between a noun and the noun that renames it, and the space before the colon is also nonstandard. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
